abstract: The electrochemical reduction of CO into value-added liquid fuels and chemicals represents a sustainable pathway for carbon recycling, yet achieving high activity at low overpotentials remains a significant challenge. Oxide-derived Cu (OD-Cu) has emerged as an exceptionally active catalyst for this process, though the specific nature of its active sites has remained unclear. In this study, we utilize temperature-programmed desorption (TPD) of CO and electron nanodiffraction to investigate the surface chemistry and microstructure of OD-Cu. We identify a unique high-temperature TPD feature on OD-Cu that is absent in conventional polycrystalline Cu foils, indicating the presence of surface sites with a significantly higher binding affinity for CO. To evaluate the role of these sites, we modulated the catalyst’s properties through thermal annealing and by varying precursor oxidation conditions. Our results show that higher annealing temperatures lead to a dramatic decrease—up to 44-fold—in the specific current density for CO reduction, which correlates directly with a loss of these strong-binding surface sites and a reduction in bulk grain boundary density. Based on these findings, we propose that metastable surface defects associated with grain boundary terminations are the primary active sites responsible for the high selectivity and efficiency of OD-Cu toward C2 products. This work establishes a link between microstructural defects and adsorbate binding thermodynamics, providing a critical foundation for the design of next-generation CO2 and CO reduction electrocatalysts.
